clinicalStem: A 58-year-old man presents to his GP with a 2-day history of sudden-onset severe pain, swelling, and redness of the right first metatarsophalangeal (MTP) joint. He cannot bear weight on the foot. He has a background of hypertension managed with bendroflumethiazide, and his eGFR is 52 mL/min/1.73m². Joint aspiration reveals negatively birefringent needle-shaped crystals. He has no history of peptic ulcer disease and is not on anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate initial pharmacological treatment for this acute attack?
options: Oral naproxen 500 mg twice daily with a proton pump inhibitor
Oral allopurinol 100 mg once daily, titrating to urate target
Intramuscular dexamethasone 8 mg stat
Oral colchicine 500 mcg twice daily
Intra-articular methylprednisolone 40 mg
explanation: In acute gout, colchicine is first-line when NSAIDs are relatively contraindicated due to reduced renal function (eGFR <60 mL/min/1.73m² requires NSAID dose reduction or avoidance). Colchicine 500 mcg twice or three times daily is effective, with dose reduction required in moderate CKD. NSAIDs are the alternative first-line in normal renal function but should be used cautiously here. Allopurinol should never be started during an acute attack as it can prolong or worsen the flare.
wrongAnswerExplanations: NSAIDs such as naproxen are a valid first-line option but should be used cautiously with an eGFR of 52 mL/min/1.73m²; colchicine is the preferred choice when renal function is impaired.
Allopurinol is a urate-lowering therapy for chronic gout prophylaxis; starting it during an acute attack can paradoxically worsen or prolong the flare and is contraindicated at this stage.
IM corticosteroids are a valid option if both NSAIDs and colchicine are contraindicated, but are not first-line when colchicine can be safely used at a reduced dose.
Colchicine 500 mcg twice daily is the correct answer — effective in acute gout with dose adjustment for moderate CKD.
Intra-articular steroids are useful when a single large joint is involved, but the first MTP joint is small and systemic colchicine is more practical and appropriate here.
clinicalPearl: Never start allopurinol during an acute gout attack — always treat the flare first, then commence urate-lowering therapy after the attack fully resolves.
keyClue: eGFR 52 mL/min/1.73m² (moderate CKD) makes NSAIDs higher risk; negatively birefringent crystals confirm gout.
commonExamTrap: Choosing naproxen when eGFR is impaired, or starting allopurinol immediately to 'treat the gout'.
missedConcept: Colchicine is preferred over NSAIDs in renal impairment; urate-lowering therapy must not be initiated mid-attack.

clinicalStem: A 62-year-old man had his second acute gout attack in 8 months, both involving the left ankle. Between attacks he is asymptomatic. His serum urate is 540 µmol/L. His acute attack has fully resolved for 3 weeks. He is keen to prevent future attacks. His GP plans to start allopurinol. Which of the following statements best describes the correct approach to initiating allopurinol in this patient?
options: Start allopurinol 300 mg daily immediately and check urate at 6 weeks
Start allopurinol 100 mg daily with colchicine 500 mcg daily co-prescribed as prophylaxis for the first 3–6 months
Start allopurinol only after a third attack, as two attacks do not yet meet criteria
Start febuxostat 80 mg daily as it is superior to allopurinol for urate lowering in all patients
Start allopurinol 300 mg daily and advise the patient no further attacks will occur within 2 weeks
explanation: Allopurinol should be started at a low dose (100 mg daily) and titrated upward every 2–4 weeks to achieve a target serum urate below 360 µmol/L (or <300 µmol/L in tophaceous disease). Crucially, a prophylactic anti-inflammatory — colchicine 500 mcg once or twice daily (or an NSAID) — should be co-prescribed for the first 3–6 months to prevent mobilisation flares triggered by rapid urate reduction. Two or more attacks within a year is a clear indication to start urate-lowering therapy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Starting at 300 mg without titration risks a larger urate drop, precipitating a mobilisation flare; low-dose initiation with prophylaxis is standard.
This is the correct answer: start low, titrate slowly, and cover with colchicine prophylaxis for 3–6 months.
Two attacks in under a year, plus a urate of 540 µmol/L, are clear indications for urate-lowering therapy; waiting for a third attack is incorrect.
Febuxostat is a second-line option (e.g., intolerance to allopurinol); allopurinol is the established first-line urate-lowering agent.
Mobilisation flares commonly occur in the first weeks of urate-lowering therapy; prophylactic colchicine — not reassurance — prevents them.
clinicalPearl: When starting allopurinol, always co-prescribe colchicine 500 mcg once or twice daily for 3–6 months to prevent mobilisation flares from rapid urate reduction.
keyClue: Attack fully resolved 3 weeks ago; two attacks in 8 months; ready to start urate-lowering therapy.
commonExamTrap: Starting allopurinol at full dose without prophylaxis, or starting it while the attack is still active.
missedConcept: Urate-lowering therapy requires low-dose titration plus concurrent anti-inflammatory prophylaxis to prevent precipitating a new flare.

clinicalStem: A 67-year-old woman with hypertension and a history of gout presents with her third gout flare in 12 months. She takes bendroflumethiazide 2.5 mg daily for blood pressure control. Her BP today is 138/86 mmHg. She is not on any urate-lowering therapy. Which of the following changes to her management would most directly address the underlying cause of her recurrent gout?
options: Add allopurinol and continue bendroflumethiazide
Switch bendroflumethiazide to losartan and start allopurinol
Increase bendroflumethiazide to 5 mg daily and add colchicine prophylaxis
Add febuxostat without changing her antihypertensive
Stop all antihypertensives to reduce urate burden
explanation: Thiazide diuretics cause hyperuricaemia by reducing renal urate excretion and are a well-recognised precipitant of gout. In a hypertensive patient with recurrent gout, switching from a thiazide to losartan is the preferred move: losartan has a mild uricosuric effect and is the antihypertensive of choice in gout. Adding allopurinol addresses the serum urate target. Together, this combination treats both the hypertension and reduces gout risk through complementary mechanisms.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Adding allopurinol without removing the thiazide leaves the main drug-induced precipitant in place; losartan is the preferred antihypertensive in gout.
Correct — switching to losartan removes the thiazide-driven hyperuricaemia, and losartan itself is uricosuric; adding allopurinol further lowers urate.
Increasing the thiazide dose would worsen hyperuricaemia and increase gout risk further.
Febuxostat would lower urate but does not address the drug-induced cause; losartan substitution is also important.
Stopping antihypertensives entirely is unsafe and unnecessary; losartan is an effective antihypertensive that also benefits urate control.
clinicalPearl: Losartan is the antihypertensive of choice in patients with gout — it has a mild uricosuric effect, in contrast to thiazides which raise serum urate.
keyClue: Bendroflumethiazide use in a patient with recurrent gout — the thiazide is the precipitant.
commonExamTrap: Adding allopurinol without removing the offending thiazide, or failing to recognise losartan's uricosuric advantage.
missedConcept: Thiazide diuretics elevate serum urate; losartan is the preferred antihypertensive in gout patients requiring BP control.

clinicalStem: A 71-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of progressive bilateral knee pain, worse on activity and relieved by rest. She describes morning stiffness lasting under 30 minutes. X-ray shows joint space narrowing, subchondral sclerosis, and osteophytes bilaterally. She has tried regular paracetamol 1 g four times daily for 6 weeks with inadequate pain relief. She has no history of peptic ulcer disease, her eGFR is 78 mL/min/1.73m², and she is not on aspirin or anticoagulation. What is the most appropriate next pharmacological step?
options: Add oral morphine sulphate 10 mg modified-release twice daily
Add topical diclofenac gel to both knees
Switch paracetamol to co-codamol 8/500 four times daily
Start oral naproxen 500 mg twice daily with a proton pump inhibitor
Stop all analgesia and refer directly for knee replacement
explanation: In osteoarthritis, topical NSAIDs (e.g., topical diclofenac) are recommended after inadequate response to paracetamol, particularly in older patients, as they provide meaningful local analgesia with significantly lower systemic side-effect risk (GI, renal, cardiovascular) compared to oral NSAIDs. NICE guidelines advocate topical NSAIDs as a preferred next step before oral NSAIDs in knee OA. Topical diclofenac is well tolerated and effective in knee OA.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Strong opioids such as morphine are not appropriate at this stage of OA management; non-opioid and topical options should be exhausted first.
Topical diclofenac is the correct next step — effective local analgesia with minimal systemic side effects, recommended before oral NSAIDs in elderly patients.
Co-codamol provides weak opioid analgesia but does not address the inflammatory component and carries constipation risk in the elderly; topical NSAIDs are preferred.
Oral NSAIDs are effective but carry greater GI and renal risk, especially in the elderly; topical NSAIDs are recommended first in knee OA.
Surgical referral is premature; pharmacological and non-pharmacological management should be optimised before considering surgery.
clinicalPearl: In knee OA, topical NSAIDs are recommended before oral NSAIDs — they achieve local therapeutic concentrations with substantially less systemic toxicity.
keyClue: Inadequate response to paracetamol in knee OA with normal renal function in an older patient — next step on the analgesic ladder.
commonExamTrap: Jumping straight to oral NSAIDs or weak opioids before trialling topical NSAIDs.
missedConcept: NICE recommends topical NSAIDs as the preferred next analgesic step after paracetamol in knee OA, ahead of oral NSAIDs.

clinicalStem: A 74-year-old man with radiologically confirmed severe right knee OA reports inadequate symptom control despite regular paracetamol, topical diclofenac, and a structured physiotherapy programme. He is awaiting a knee replacement. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus managed with metformin and sitagliptin, with an HbA1c of 58 mmol/mol. He requests an injection to help with pain in the interim. Which of the following intra-articular treatments is most appropriate?
options: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid (viscosupplementation) injection
Intra-articular methylprednisolone 40 mg injection with a caution regarding transient glycaemic effect
Intra-articular methylprednisolone 40 mg injection — diabetes is not a concern at standard doses
Intra-articular platelet-rich plasma injection as first-line bridging therapy
Oral prednisolone 30 mg for 5 days as equivalent systemic bridging therapy
explanation: Intra-articular corticosteroid injections (e.g., methylprednisolone 40 mg) are recommended for short-term pain relief in OA while awaiting surgery. They are appropriate in this patient but should be accompanied by a specific caution: corticosteroids — even intra-articular — can cause transient hyperglycaemia in diabetic patients for 24–72 hours post-injection. The patient should be counselled to monitor his glucose closely after the injection. Hyaluronic acid (viscosupplementation) is no longer recommended by NICE due to insufficient evidence of benefit.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Intra-articular hyaluronic acid is not recommended by current NICE guidelines for knee OA due to lack of clinically meaningful benefit over placebo.
This is correct — intra-articular corticosteroid is appropriate bridging analgesia, but the diabetic patient must be warned about transient blood glucose elevation post-injection.
Diabetes is a significant consideration: even intra-articular steroids can elevate blood glucose for 24–72 hours; omitting this counselling is an error.
Platelet-rich plasma is not a standard recommended treatment in current UK guidelines for OA.
Systemic oral prednisolone carries far greater risks of glycaemic disruption and other steroid side effects in a diabetic patient; intra-articular delivery is preferred.
clinicalPearl: Intra-articular corticosteroids can cause transient hyperglycaemia for 24–72 hours in diabetic patients — always counsel accordingly and advise extra glucose monitoring.
keyClue: Diabetic patient awaiting knee replacement requiring bridging analgesia — steroid injection is appropriate but requires glycaemic counselling.
commonExamTrap: Choosing hyaluronic acid injection (not recommended by NICE) or failing to flag the glycaemic risk of intra-articular steroids in diabetes.
missedConcept: Intra-articular steroids remain the preferred injection for OA bridging; hyaluronic acid is not NICE-approved; glycaemia must be discussed in diabetic patients.

clinicalStem: A 44-year-old woman with seropositive rheumatoid arthritis (RF positive, anti-CCP positive) was started on methotrexate 10 mg weekly 8 weeks ago. She attends for routine monitoring. She is a non-smoker and takes folic acid 5 mg weekly (on a different day to methotrexate). Today's blood results show: ALT 98 U/L (normal <40), WBC 3.1 × 10⁹/L (normal 4–11), platelets 210 × 10⁹/L. She feels well. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Continue methotrexate at the current dose; repeat bloods in 4 weeks as ALT elevation is expected
Withhold methotrexate and arrange urgent rheumatology review given elevated ALT and low WBC
Increase folic acid to 5 mg daily (every day except methotrexate day) and continue current dose
Switch immediately to leflunomide as methotrexate is causing toxicity
Continue methotrexate and increase the dose to 15 mg to improve efficacy
explanation: Methotrexate requires close monitoring for hepatotoxicity (raised transaminases) and myelosuppression (low WBC/neutrophils, thrombocytopenia). A WBC of 3.1 × 10⁹/L represents leucopenia and, combined with an ALT more than twice the upper limit of normal, constitutes significant toxicity requiring immediate withholding of the drug and urgent specialist input. Continuing or increasing the dose with either abnormality alone — let alone both — risks serious harm. These are recognised thresholds for stopping methotrexate according to monitoring guidelines.
wrongAnswerExplanations: ALT elevation to more than twice ULN and a low WBC are not expected or acceptable findings; they require methotrexate to be withheld, not continued.
Correct — both the raised ALT (>2× ULN) and the leucopenia are methotrexate toxicity signals; the drug must be withheld and rheumatology urgently contacted.
Increasing folic acid frequency is sometimes done to reduce side effects, but the current results represent objective toxicity requiring methotrexate to be stopped, not continued.
Switching to leflunomide may be appropriate long-term, but this decision requires specialist input; immediate action is to withhold methotrexate and escalate.
Increasing the dose is absolutely contraindicated when the patient already shows hepatotoxicity and leucopenia from the current dose.
clinicalPearl: Withhold methotrexate if ALT >2× ULN or WBC <3.5 × 10⁹/L — both findings together mandate stopping the drug and urgent rheumatology review.
keyClue: ALT >2× ULN plus WBC <3.5 × 10⁹/L on methotrexate = significant toxicity requiring drug withdrawal.
commonExamTrap: Assuming minor transaminase rises are 'expected' with methotrexate and continuing treatment despite monitoring thresholds being breached.
missedConcept: Methotrexate monitoring thresholds exist for safety; exceeding them requires withholding the drug — not dose adjustment or reassurance.

clinicalStem: A 52-year-old man with established seropositive rheumatoid arthritis has inadequate disease control despite 18 months of combination methotrexate and hydroxychloroquine at maximally tolerated doses. His DAS28 score is 5.8. He is referred to rheumatology for consideration of biologic therapy. His past medical history includes latent tuberculosis treated with isoniazid 9 months ago (completed), well-controlled heart failure with an ejection fraction of 28%, and a remote history of hepatitis B (HBsAg negative, anti-HBc positive, HBV DNA undetectable). Which of the following would most strongly contraindicate the use of a TNF-alpha inhibitor in this patient?
options: Seropositive RA with anti-CCP antibodies
Previous latent TB treated with isoniazid 9 months ago
Heart failure with ejection fraction of 28%
Resolved hepatitis B with undetectable HBV DNA
DAS28 score of 5.8 indicating high disease activity
explanation: TNF-alpha inhibitors (e.g., etanercept, adalimumab, infliximab) are contraindicated in moderate-to-severe heart failure (NYHA class III–IV or ejection fraction <35–40%), as they worsen cardiac function and increase mortality in this setting. This is a firm contraindication. Latent TB requires prophylaxis before TNF-inhibitor use but, if fully treated, is not an ongoing contraindication. Resolved hepatitis B requires monitoring and antiviral prophylaxis during biologic therapy. High DAS28 is an indication for, not a contraindication to, biologics.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Seropositive RA is an indication for biologic therapy — seropositivity does not contraindicate treatment.
Completed latent TB prophylaxis (isoniazid for 9 months) generally satisfies the requirement before TNF-inhibitor initiation; active TB, not treated latent TB, is the contraindication.
Correct — EF 28% constitutes severe heart failure (NYHA class III–IV equivalent); TNF-alpha inhibitors are contraindicated as they have been shown to worsen cardiac outcomes in this setting.

Resolved hepatitis B (HBsAg negative, HBV DNA undetectable) is not a contraindication but does require regular viral load monitoring and possibly prophylactic antiviral therapy during biologic treatment.
A DAS28 of 5.8 indicates high disease activity — this is the clinical justification for escalating to biologic therapy, not a barrier to it.
clinicalPearl: TNF-alpha inhibitors are contraindicated in moderate-to-severe heart failure (EF <35–40%) — they worsen cardiac function and increase mortality in this setting.
keyClue: Ejection fraction of 28% = severe systolic heart failure — a firm contraindication to TNF-alpha inhibitor use.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on the history of latent TB as the contraindication when completed prophylaxis clears this; missing that severe heart failure is the definitive bar.
missedConcept: TNF-alpha inhibitors worsen heart failure outcomes and are contraindicated in moderate-to-severe HF; completed TB prophylaxis is not a lasting contraindication.

clinicalStem: A 31-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis is planning a pregnancy. She is currently well controlled on methotrexate 15 mg weekly and folic acid. She asks about her medications. Regarding her drug management, which of the following is the most important recommendation to make?
options: Continue methotrexate throughout pregnancy as the dose is low; add extra folic acid 5 mg daily
Stop methotrexate at least 3 months before conception and switch to an appropriate alternative such as sulfasalazine or hydroxychloroquine
Switch from methotrexate to leflunomide, which is safe in pregnancy
Stop all DMARDs 6 weeks before trying to conceive to allow the immune system to recover
Continue methotrexate but add low-dose prednisolone as a bridging agent throughout the first trimester
explanation: Methotrexate is absolutely teratogenic (folate antagonist causing neural tube defects, craniofacial anomalies, limb defects) and must be stopped at least 3 months (some guidelines say 6 months) before attempting conception. It is also a category X drug in pregnancy. Appropriate DMARD alternatives that are compatible with pregnancy include sulfasalazine (with high-dose folic acid) and hydroxychloroquine. Leflunomide is also teratogenic and requires a washout procedure before pregnancy. Stopping all DMARDs risks disease flare, which itself is harmful in pregnancy.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Methotrexate is absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy and pre-conception — it is a potent teratogen regardless of dose; extra folic acid does not negate this risk.
Correct — methotrexate must be stopped at least 3 months before trying to conceive; sulfasalazine and hydroxychloroquine are safe alternatives for maintaining RA control during pregnancy.
Leflunomide is also teratogenic; switching to it would not make the patient safer — a washout procedure is required before pregnancy can be attempted on leflunomide.
Stopping all DMARDs risks an RA flare during pregnancy; pregnancy-compatible options (hydroxychloroquine, sulfasalazine) should replace methotrexate, not nothing.
Continuing methotrexate in any trimester is contraindicated; low-dose prednisolone can be used for flares in pregnancy but does not replace the need to stop methotrexate before conception.
clinicalPearl: Methotrexate is absolutely teratogenic — stop at least 3 months before conception and switch to a pregnancy-safe DMARD (hydroxychloroquine or sulfasalazine).
keyClue: Woman on methotrexate planning pregnancy — teratogenicity mandates pre-conception drug change.
commonExamTrap: Thinking folic acid supplementation makes methotrexate safe in pregnancy, or suggesting leflunomide as a safe alternative.
missedConcept: Both methotrexate and leflunomide are teratogenic and contraindicated pre-conceptually; hydroxychloroquine and sulfasalazine are the safe DMARD options in pregnancy.

clinicalStem: A 60-year-old woman with rheumatoid arthritis has been taking prednisolone 10 mg daily for 18 months as a bridging agent while her DMARDs were being optimised. She now attends her GP for routine review. She has no known osteoporosis and is not on any bone protection. Which of the following complications of long-term glucocorticoid therapy is she most at risk of that the GP must address today?
options: Adrenal insufficiency if steroids are stopped abruptly
Cushing syndrome with central adiposity, striae, and hypertension
Glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis requiring bisphosphonate therapy
Steroid-induced psychosis requiring psychiatric referral
Cataracts requiring ophthalmology screening
explanation: Any patient on oral glucocorticoids at a dose of 7.5 mg prednisolone or more daily for 3 months or longer should be offered bone protection with a bisphosphonate (e.g., alendronate) and calcium/vitamin D supplementation to prevent glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis (GIOP). This is a specific, actionable, and NICE-mandated intervention. This patient has been on 10 mg for 18 months without any bone protection — this is a significant clinical oversight that requires immediate correction. All other listed complications exist but the bone protection gap is the most urgent, addressable, guideline-mandated issue today.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Adrenal insufficiency on abrupt cessation is a real risk and dose tapering is important, but the most urgent unaddressed gap today is the absence of bone protection.
Cushingoid features are a recognised complication but require no immediate specific prescription beyond addressing reversible risk factors; osteoporosis prevention is more actionable.
Correct — 10 mg prednisolone daily for 18 months mandates bisphosphonate plus calcium/vitamin D prescription; GIOP is preventable and this omission must be corrected today.
Steroid psychosis is rare at low doses and not the priority to address routinely in this otherwise well patient.
Posterior subcapsular cataracts are associated with long-term steroids but ophthalmology monitoring, while advisable, is less immediately actionable than prescribing bone protection.
clinicalPearl: All patients taking prednisolone ≥7.5 mg/day for ≥3 months should be prescribed bone protection (bisphosphonate + calcium/vitamin D) to prevent glucocorticoid-induced osteoporosis.
keyClue: 18 months of prednisolone 10 mg daily with no bone protection prescribed — GIOP prevention is the most urgent unaddressed issue.
commonExamTrap: Focusing on Cushingoid features or adrenal suppression rather than the guideline-mandated and easily correctable absence of bisphosphonate.
missedConcept: GIOP is the most common preventable harm from long-term steroids; bisphosphonate therapy must be initiated early in all eligible patients.

clinicalStem: A 29-year-old woman is newly diagnosed with systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE) based on arthralgia, a photosensitive malar rash, positive ANA (1:320) and anti-dsDNA antibodies, and mild proteinuria. She has no major organ involvement. Her ophthalmology review is normal. The rheumatologist plans to start hydroxychloroquine. Which of the following statements most accurately describes the role and monitoring requirement for hydroxychloroquine in SLE?
options: Hydroxychloroquine is used only for acute flares and should be stopped once symptoms resolve
Hydroxychloroquine reduces flare frequency, lowers cardiovascular risk, and requires annual ophthalmology review after 5 years of use due to retinal toxicity risk
Hydroxychloroquine should be used only in patients with severe renal lupus to spare corticosteroids
Hydroxychloroquine is contraindicated in women of childbearing age due to teratogenicity
Hydroxychloroquine provides immunosuppression equivalent to azathioprine and does not require any monitoring
explanation: Hydroxychloroquine is recommended for virtually all SLE patients, regardless of disease severity, as it reduces flare frequency, prevents organ damage, lowers thrombosis risk (important given SLE's pro-thrombotic state), improves survival, and is safe in pregnancy. The key monitoring requirement is annual ophthalmology screening after 5 years of use (or earlier if risk factors for retinal toxicity exist), as hydroxychloroquine can cause irreversible retinal/macular toxicity (bull's-eye maculopathy) at cumulative doses.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hydroxychloroquine is a long-term maintenance agent in SLE — it should be continued indefinitely, not stopped when symptoms resolve.

Correct — hydroxychloroquine is used as a background therapy in nearly all SLE patients; its benefits include flare reduction and cardiovascular protection; ophthalmology review is required after 5 years.
Hydroxychloroquine is appropriate across all disease severities in SLE, not only severe renal disease; it is often the cornerstone of treatment in mild-to-moderate disease.
Hydroxychloroquine is actually one of the few immunomodulatory agents considered safe in pregnancy and is often continued throughout; it is not teratogenic.
Hydroxychloroquine is an antimalarial with immunomodulatory effects — less potent than azathioprine — and retinal monitoring is essential given cumulative toxicity risk.
clinicalPearl: Hydroxychloroquine should be offered to virtually all SLE patients long-term; annual ophthalmology review for retinal toxicity is required after 5 years of use.
keyClue: Newly diagnosed SLE with mild-moderate features — hydroxychloroquine is the universal background therapy with a specific retinal toxicity monitoring requirement.
commonExamTrap: Thinking hydroxychloroquine is contraindicated in pregnancy, or that ophthalmology review is not needed because the baseline was normal.
missedConcept: Hydroxychloroquine benefits all SLE patients (not just severe disease), is safe in pregnancy, and requires retinal monitoring after 5 years of cumulative use.

clinicalStem: A 34-year-old woman with known SLE presents with 3 weeks of worsening ankle oedema, frothy urine, and fatigue. Urinalysis shows 3+ protein and active urinary sediment (red cell casts). Her BP is 148/94 mmHg. Creatinine is 132 µmol/L (baseline 72 µmol/L). Anti-dsDNA titre is markedly elevated. Renal biopsy confirms class III/IV proliferative lupus nephritis. Which of the following is the most appropriate induction immunosuppression regimen?
options: Oral hydroxychloroquine 400 mg daily alone
High-dose oral prednisolone with mycophenolate mofetil (MMF) or IV cyclophosphamide
Azathioprine and prednisolone 10 mg daily as first-line induction
Rituximab monotherapy as preferred first-line induction in proliferative lupus nephritis
Oral methotrexate and low-dose prednisolone
explanation: Class III/IV proliferative lupus nephritis is a serious, organ-threatening manifestation of SLE requiring aggressive induction therapy. Current guidelines (ACR, EULAR) recommend high-dose corticosteroids combined with either mycophenolate mofetil (MMF, 2–3 g/day) or IV cyclophosphamide as induction therapy. MMF is preferred in many centres due to comparable efficacy and a more favourable side-effect profile. Hydroxychloroquine alone is insufficient for active nephritis. Azathioprine is used for maintenance, not induction. Methotrexate is avoided in renal impairment and has no role in nephritis induction.
wrongAnswerExplanations: Hydroxychloroquine is a background therapy in all SLE patients but is wholly insufficient as sole treatment for class III/IV lupus nephritis.
Correct — high-dose corticosteroids plus MMF or IV cyclophosphamide is the standard induction regimen for proliferative lupus nephritis according to ACR/EULAR guidelines.
Azathioprine is used for maintenance therapy after induction, not as an induction agent for proliferative nephritis; it is less potent and evidence does not support it for induction.
Rituximab is considered in refractory lupus nephritis after failure of standard induction; it is not the preferred first-line induction agent.
Methotrexate is renally cleared and contraindicated in significant renal impairment; it has no role in lupus nephritis induction.
clinicalPearl: Class III/IV proliferative lupus nephritis requires induction with high-dose steroids plus MMF or IV cyclophosphamide — azathioprine is for maintenance only.
keyClue: Red cell casts + rising creatinine + elevated anti-dsDNA in SLE = proliferative lupus nephritis requiring aggressive immunosuppressive induction.
commonExamTrap: Using azathioprine for induction (it is maintenance therapy) or offering hydroxychloroquine as the primary treatment for severe nephritis.
missedConcept: Induction vs maintenance is a critical distinction in lupus nephritis: MMF or cyclophosphamide for induction; azathioprine or MMF for maintenance.

clinicalStem: A 38-year-old woman with SLE presents following her second unprovoked deep vein thrombosis (DVT). She was anticoagulated with warfarin after her first DVT 2 years ago but stopped it after 6 months. Investigations now show: lupus anticoagulant positive on two occasions 12 weeks apart, anticardiolipin IgG antibody titre >40 U/mL, anti-β2-glycoprotein I positive. She is haemodynamically stable. What is the most appropriate long-term management of her thromboembolic risk?
options: Warfarin with a target INR of 2–3 indefinitely
Apixaban 2.5 mg twice daily as a DOAC — equivalent to warfarin in APS
Warfarin with a target INR of 3–4 indefinitely given triple-positive antiphospholipid antibodies
Aspirin 75 mg daily as primary thromboprophylaxis
Repeat anticoagulation with warfarin for 12 months then reassess
explanation: This patient has definite antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) — two thromboses and triple-positive antiphospholipid antibodies — which defines her as high thrombotic risk. For patients with triple-positive APS and recurrent thrombosis, current EULAR guidelines recommend indefinite warfarin anticoagulation with a higher target INR of 3–4 (rather than the standard 2–3) to prevent further events. DOACs, particularly rivaroxaban, have been shown to be inferior to warfarin in high-risk APS (TRAPS and RAPS trials), with higher rates of recurrent thrombosis; they are not recommended in triple-positive APS.
wrongAnswerExplanations: A standard INR target of 2–3 may be insufficient for triple-positive APS with recurrent thrombosis; high-risk APS warrants a higher INR target of 3–4.
DOACs are contraindicated in high-risk (triple-positive) APS — clinical trials (TRAPS trial) showed DOACs are inferior to warfarin in this group, with higher recurrence rates.
Correct — triple-positive APS with recurrent thrombosis requires indefinite warfarin with a target INR of 3–4 per EULAR guidance.
Aspirin alone is used for thromboprophylaxis in APS patients with obstetric complications only (no thrombosis history); it is wholly inadequate following two DVTs.
Stopping anticoagulation after 12 months is inappropriate — triple-positive APS with recurrent thrombosis is an indefinite anticoagulation indication.
clinicalPearl: Triple-positive APS with recurrent thrombosis warrants indefinite warfarin at a higher INR target (3–4); DOACs are inferior and not recommended in high-risk APS.
keyClue: Triple-positive antiphospholipid antibodies + second DVT = high-risk APS requiring indefinite warfarin with INR target 3–4.
commonExamTrap: Prescribing a DOAC (apixaban/rivaroxaban) in high-risk APS, or using a standard INR 2–3 target when the higher range is indicated.
missedConcept: DOACs are not safe in triple-positive APS — warfarin with higher INR target (3–4) is mandated; the risk of DOAC use here has trial-level evidence of harm.
